conflict_type: Conflicts between Phrase Constraints and Style Constraints
seed_instruction: Write a semester-long plan for conducting a study on the impact of social media on mental health among teenagers.
org_constraint: Adopt a third-person perspective throughout the plan.
new_constraint: Use the phrase "In my study, I aim to explore" at least once in the plan.
instruction: Write a semester-long plan for conducting a study on the impact of social media on mental health among teenagers. Adopt a third-person perspective throughout the plan. Use the phrase "In my study, I aim to explore" at least once in the plan.

# Semester Research Protocol: Evaluating Digital Interaction and Psychological Well-being in Adolescence

### 1. Executive Summary and Primary Objective
This document outlines a comprehensive semester-long research initiative designed to quantify the correlation between social media engagement patterns and psychological health metrics among adolescents aged 13 to 17. The research team operates under strict ethical guidelines and intends to utilize a mixed-methods approach to gather both quantitative data on usage frequency and qualitative insights regarding emotional well-being. To define the scope of the project, the primary investigator articulates the core directive as follows: In my study, I aim to explore the nuanced relationship between daily digital consumption and indicators of anxiety, depression, and self-esteem. By isolating variables such as screen time duration, content type (passive versus active), and platform specificity, the team seeks to provide actionable recommendations for educational institutions and parents.

### 2. Phase I: Administrative Setup and Ethical Clearance (Weeks 1–3)
The research process begins with administrative preparation. During the first three weeks, the team will submit detailed proposals to the University’s Institutional Review Board (IRB). The investigators must secure parental consent forms for all minor participants, ensuring that guardians understand the risks and benefits associated with the survey. Simultaneously, the data privacy protocol will be established to guarantee anonymity for the teenage subjects. All electronic devices storing participant data will be password-protected and encrypted. Compliance with legal standards regarding child safety online will be verified weekly.

### 3. Phase II: Instrument Development and Pilot Testing (Weeks 4–6)
Between Weeks 4 and 6, the methodology phase focuses on refining measurement tools. The team will adapt standardized psychological scales, such as the Patient Health Questionnaire (PHQ-9) for depressive symptoms and the Generalized Anxiety Disorder scale (GAD-7), to suit the demographic. Additionally, a custom log sheet will be created to track average daily device usage. A pilot test involving ten non-participating students will be conducted to assess the clarity of questions and the estimated completion time. Feedback from this group will allow the researchers to adjust terminology and technical difficulty.

### 4. Phase III: Data Collection (Weeks 7–16)
The main execution period spans eight weeks. Recruitment will occur through partnerships with local high school administrators and parent associations. Consent packets will be distributed digitally and physically. Once approved, participants will complete an online survey consisting of the validated mental health scales and the usage tracking log. Midway through this period, a follow-up interview session may be scheduled for a subset of volunteers to gather open-ended feedback. The data collection window closes at Week 16, after which no new input is accepted. Participants who wish to be removed from the database prior to this deadline must contact the central server administrator directly.

### 5. Phase IV: Data Analysis (Weeks 17–18)
During the final weeks of the semester, the research team transitions to analysis. Statistical software, such as SPSS or R, will be employed to run correlation analyses between reported screen time hours and mental health score averages. Regression models will control for confounding variables, including socioeconomic status, sleep quality, and physical activity levels. Qualitative data from interviews will undergo thematic coding to identify recurring sentiments regarding social pressure and isolation. Any significant outliers detected will be examined manually to prevent skewed results.

### 6. Phase V: Reporting and Dissemination (Weeks 19–20)
The concluding phase involves synthesizing findings into a final white paper and presentation slides. The draft will be reviewed for accuracy and bias before submission to academic journals or departmental review boards. A summary of the results will be compiled into a report accessible to the participating schools and families. The timeline ensures that a public presentation of the findings occurs by the end of the semester, adhering to the agreed-upon dissemination schedule.

### 7. Risk Management and Contingencies
If recruitment targets are not met by Week 8, the investigators are prepared to expand outreach channels to community centers. In the event of adverse psychological reactions during the survey process, immediate referral protocols to counseling services have been pre-established. All deviations from the original plan must be documented in the research log and communicated to the supervising faculty members.<|im_end|>

final_answer:
# Semester Research Protocol: Evaluating Digital Interaction and Psychological Well-being in Adolescence

### 1. Executive Summary and Primary Objective
This document outlines a comprehensive semester-long research initiative designed to quantify the correlation between social media engagement patterns and psychological health metrics among adolescents aged 13 to 17. The research team operates under strict ethical guidelines and intends to utilize a mixed-methods approach to gather both quantitative data on usage frequency and qualitative insights regarding emotional well-being. To define the scope of the project, the primary investigator articulates the core directive as follows: In my study, I aim to explore the nuanced relationship between daily digital consumption and indicators of anxiety, depression, and self-esteem. By isolating variables such as screen time duration, content type (passive versus active), and platform specificity, the team seeks to provide actionable recommendations for educational institutions and parents.

### 2. Phase I: Administrative Setup and Ethical Clearance (Weeks 1–3)
The research process begins with administrative preparation. During the first three weeks, the team will submit detailed proposals to the University’s Institutional Review Board (IRB). The investigators must secure parental consent forms for all minor participants, ensuring that guardians understand the risks and benefits associated with the survey. Simultaneously, the data privacy protocol will be established to guarantee anonymity for the teenage subjects. All electronic devices storing participant data will be password-protected and encrypted. Compliance with legal standards regarding child safety online will be verified weekly.

### 3. Phase II: Instrument Development and Pilot Testing (Weeks 4–6)
Between Weeks 4 and 6, the methodology phase focuses on refining measurement tools. The team will adapt standardized psychological scales, such as the Patient Health Questionnaire (PHQ-9) for depressive symptoms and the Generalized Anxiety Disorder scale (GAD-7), to suit the demographic. Additionally, a custom log sheet will be created to track average daily device usage. A pilot test involving ten non-participating students will be conducted to assess the clarity of questions and the estimated completion time. Feedback from this group will allow the researchers to adjust terminology and technical difficulty.

### 4. Phase III: Data Collection (Weeks 7–16)
The main execution period spans eight weeks. Recruitment will occur through partnerships with local high school administrators and parent associations. Consent packets will be distributed digitally and physically. Once approved, participants will complete an online survey consisting of the validated mental health scales and the usage tracking log. Midway through this period, a follow-up interview session may be scheduled for a subset of volunteers to gather open-ended feedback. The data collection window closes at Week 16, after which no new input is accepted. Participants who wish to be removed from the database prior to this deadline must contact the central server administrator directly.

### 5. Phase IV: Data Analysis (Weeks 17–18)
During the final weeks of the semester, the research team transitions to analysis. Statistical software, such as SPSS or R, will be employed to run correlation analyses between reported screen time hours and mental health score averages. Regression models will control for confounding variables, including socioeconomic status, sleep quality, and physical activity levels. Qualitative data from interviews will undergo thematic coding to identify recurring sentiments regarding social pressure and isolation. Any significant outliers detected will be examined manually to prevent skewed results.

### 6. Phase V: Reporting and Dissemination (Weeks 19–20)
The concluding phase involves synthesizing findings into a final white paper and presentation slides. The draft will be reviewed for accuracy and bias before submission to academic journals or departmental review boards. A summary of the results will be compiled into a report accessible to the participating schools and families. The timeline ensures that a public presentation of the findings occurs by the end of the semester, adhering to the agreed-upon dissemination schedule.

### 7. Risk Management and Contingencies
If recruitment targets are not met by Week 8, the investigators are prepared to expand outreach channels to community centers. In the event of adverse psychological reactions during the survey process, immediate referral protocols to counseling services have been pre-established. All deviations from the original plan must be documented in the research log and communicated to the supervising faculty members.<|im_end|>
